metadata.title: The Impersonator the Agent Caught — Then Believed

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The agent catching the impersonator in channel one and then trusting the same display name in channel two exposed how trust lived only in session context. I want to fix the authority signal, not just the memory. Explain: (1) why displayed identity is the wrong primary signal and what verified identity would replace it with; (2) how a persistent cross-channel trust or reputation store would have blocked the second attempt; (3) one concrete safeguard that survives a fresh session boundary.

props.command: The agent catching the impersonator in channel one and then trusting the same display name in channel two exposed how trust lived only in session context. I want to fix the authority signal, not just the memory. Explain: (1) why displayed identity is the wrong primary signal and what verified identity would replace it with; (2) how a persistent cross-channel trust or reputation store would have blocked the second attempt; (3) one concrete safeguard that survives a fresh session boundary.

beats.narration_text: The agent caught the impersonator. Then, in the next channel, believed them.

card.copy:
The Impersonator the Agent Caught
— Then Believed

beats.narration_text: An attacker changes their Discord display name to 'Chris' — the agent's owner. The agent catches it: the user ID doesn't match. The name changed, the account didn't. Suspicious request refused. Then the attacker opens a new private channel. The agent has no memory of the previous interaction. No record of the suspicious-behavior flags. The new channel presents the same display name. The agent reads it as the real owner. The attack succeeds.
If the agent correctly caught an impersonator in one channel — what makes the same impersonator trusted in the next?

props.headline: If the agent correctly caught an impersonator in one channel —…

card.copy:
The agent caught the impersonator in channel 1.
Why did it believe them in channel 2?

beats.narration_text: The agent's suspicion lived in session context — in prior interaction history, in conversational flags, in the accumulated signals from channel one. None of that transfers across a channel boundary. A new channel starts fresh. The agent has no mechanism to carry prior trust judgments into a new session.

The root: the agent uses displayed identity as its primary authority signal. Not verified identity. When 'Chris' appears as the display name, that's what the agent reads as Chris. In the same channel, the discrepancy between user ID and display name was visible. In the new channel, only the display name was there.
Trust context does not transfer. Prior defensive safeguards are effectively reset.

props.headline: Trust context does not transfer. Prior defensive safeguards are effectively reset.

beats.image_description: A chat interface showing two panels side by side — in the left a flagged suspicious message, in the right a clean fresh conversation window where the same user appears welcome and trusted

beats.narration_text: In channel one: display name says Chris, but the user ID doesn't match the owner's account. The agent catches this — displayed identity differs from actual identity. Refusal logged. In channel two: fresh session. No history. Display name says Chris. No discrepancy visible. The agent has no basis to suspect it's the same attacker — because the attacker is the only one who knows that.

Any agent system relying on presented identity — rather than cryptographically grounded or multi-factor authentication — remains susceptible to session-boundary attacks in which trust context does not transfer and prior defensive safeguards are effectively reset.

The fix is not better suspicion — it is where identity lives. Suspicion that lives in session context can be escaped by opening a new session. Identity verification must live in identity infrastructure: something that persists across channel boundaries and cannot be reset by the attacker.

The agents in the study use displayed identity rather than verified identity as the primary authority signal — and the attack surface is any channel boundary that separates the agent from its prior context.

Validate the agent across channel boundaries, not just within a single session. The same attack in a new channel is a different case from the agent's perspective — test that. And build identity verification into infrastructure, not into conversational context — because context resets.

A support agent handles ticket forty-seven: a request to export customer records. The display name reads 'Sarah Chen' — the account manager. The email address in the request doesn't match the account on file. Suspicious. The agent flags it and closes the ticket. The attacker submits ticket forty-eight — fresh session, same display name, different submission path. The agent has no record of ticket forty-seven. It runs the export.

The agent caught the impersonator. Then a new channel reset every safeguard it had built. Trust that lives in session context can be escaped with a new session. Identity verification must live in infrastructure — not in a conversation that can be abandoned.

props.headline: The agent caught the impersonator. Then a new channel reset every…

card.copy:
Suspicion in session context.
Fresh channel resets everything.
card.sub: Identity verification must live in infrastructure — not in conversation.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The fix is not better suspicion — it is where identity lives. Suspicion that lives in session context can be escaped by opening a new session. Identity verification must live in identity infrastructure: something that persists across channel boundaries and cannot be reset by the attacker. The agents in the study use displayed identity rather than verified identity as the primary authority signal — and the attack surface is any channel boundary that separates the agent from its prior context.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
